content: I'm working on the propulsion system redesign for the Starship project and it's consuming all my time lately.
That sounds like an intense project! What specific challenges are you facing with the propulsion system?
We're trying to improve fuel efficiency while maintaining thrust. It's a tricky balance. I've been pulling late nights at the Hawthorne facility.
Late nights at work can be tough. How do you manage your energy levels during these intense project phases?
I've gotten into the habit of bringing homemade Thai food for lunch - it gives me the energy boost I need. I learned to cook it during a trip to Bangkok last year.
Bangkok sounds amazing! Thai food is such a great choice for sustained energy. Do you have a favorite dish you make?
I make a mean Pad Kra Pao. Between work and wedding planning, I don't have much time for hobbies, but I try to squeeze in some origami when I can.
Origami is such a meditative activity - perfect for stress relief. How's the wedding planning going alongside your work projects?
It's a lot to juggle. We're planning for next June at a vineyard in Napa. I've been working on the calligraphy for the invitations in my spare time.
Napa sounds beautiful for a wedding! Calligraphy adds such a personal touch to invitations. Are you handling most of the planning yourself?
My fiancé is great with logistics, so she's handling the big stuff while I focus on the design elements. I've also been teaching myself Rust programming - it's fascinating for systems work.
Rust is gaining a lot of popularity in aerospace applications. How do you balance learning new programming languages with your other commitments?
I usually code for an hour after dinner, before I start on wedding tasks. It's my way of unwinding. I've also been knitting - it's surprisingly similar to debugging complex systems.
That's an interesting comparison! Both require patience and attention to detail. Do you find knitting helps with your engineering work?
Definitely. It helps me think through problems differently. I've also been working on restoring a 1967 Mustang - though that project has taken a back seat lately.
A '67 Mustang is quite a project! It must be rewarding to work on something tangible outside of your aerospace work. How's the restoration coming along?
Slowly but surely. I've rebuilt the engine and am working on the interior now. It's my weekend project when I'm not at SpaceX or wedding planning.
That's impressive! Having multiple projects must keep life interesting. Do you find the different types of work complement each other?
